en.extract:
The grey-legged tinamou (Crypturellus duidae), alternatively, the gray-legged tinamou, is a small ground-dwelling bird endemic to the neotropics. It is a rarely seen bird due to its small size and discreet appearance.


Taxonomy
The grey-legged tinamou was first described by John T. Zimmer in 1938. The grey-legged tinamou, like all tinamous, is in the family tinamidae and the infraclass palaeognathae. In addition, There are 21 species in the genus Crypterellus, all of which are all South American tinamous. There are no subspecies of the grey-legged tinamou; it is monotypic.  The genus name, Crypterellus, is formed from three latin or greek words ‘krusptis’ meaning covered or hidden, ‘oura’ meaning tail and 'ellus' meaning diminutive. Therefore, crypterullus means small hidden tail.
There is much debate in the scientific community about the membership of tinamous to the ratite clade. Ratites are an order of large flightless birds that include ostrichs, kiwis, emus, and the extinct moa and elephant bird. Tinamous were historically considered a sister clade to ratites because they did not share the flightless quality of the rest of the group. However, some genetic evidence points to tinamous being close relatives of Moas, indicating that tinamous are also members of the ratite clade. This phylogeny suggests a novel method of the evolution of flightlessness in ratites and means that flightlessness was lost multiple times among different ratites. This taxonomy is, however, unresolved and requires further investigation for a conclusive answer.


Description
The grey-legged tinamou is a small ground-dwelling bird and a weak flyer. The grey-legged tinamou has a rusty coloured neck and upper back with a tinge of grey on its upper breast. The wings and the body of the tinamou are a scaly brownish black. The grey-legged tinamou has a pronounced black eye. They vary from 28-31 cm in height. Males have a prominent dark crown of feathers on the top of the head, whereas females have a fully rusty coloured head. Females have markings on their backs and wings. The legs are a slate grey coloured which is described in the name of the species. 
The grey-legged tinamou can be misidentified with the variegated tinamou. However, the variegated tinamou has a completely grey head and bolder barring on the wings and back.


Distribution and habitat
Grey-legged tinamous are native to South America, including Columbia, Peru, Ecuador, Guyana, Venezuela, and northern Brazil. However, its distribution could be even further as the population is sparsely distributed and found in fragmented landscape. It is found in dry shrubland up to 500 m (1,600 ft) in altitude. As a poor soil specialist, the grey-legged tinamous are commonly found in Amazonian white-sand forests. These forests are characterized by low-hanging canopies and dense foliage, allowing the grey-legged tinamous to remain inconspicuous. They have also been documented in peatland habitats.


Behavior


Vocalization
The call of the grey-legged tinamou is described as a short hollow sounding two-syllable whistle, with the pneumonic: “whoo-whoooooooo” with a slight rise near the end .. The call lasts 2-3 seconds. This is the only vocalization of the grey-legged tinamou recorded. See Grey-legged tinamou call eBird. Their call is most frequently at dawn and dusk, but also during the day when it is cloudy or rainy.


Diet
The grey-legged tinamou primarily consumes fruits and other plant matter. They feed primarily on low hanging or fallen fruit. Like all tinamous they forage on the ground and in low bushes. They have also been documented to consume insects and other ground-dwelling invertebrates . 


Reproduction
Although there is no available information on the reproduction of the grey-legged tinamou, most tinamou species are ground nesters, typically choosing to nest in the depressions of the buttress roots of trees.


Conservation
In 2020 the IUCN classified the grey-legged tinamou as Least Concern, although there were calls to reclassified the species due to global population declines in the last two years in certain parts of their range. This is primarily due to increased threat from deforestation. The species is also threatened by hunting practices in the area. Indeed the regions the grey-legged tinamou is most commonly found is facing significant anthropogenic changes. although due to their large fragmented range there was yet to be detailed survey of their populations across their entire range.


References


External links
Further reading, "DNA confirms relationship between the giant flightless moa and the tinamous". Royal Ontario Museum. Retrieved 2021-11-11.
The vocalization of the grey-legged tinamou  - Macauley Library, Cornell University
Grey-legged tinamou photo gallery, -Macauley Library, Cornell University

The '''grey-legged tinamou''' ('''''Crypturellus duidae'''''), alternatively, the gray-legged tinamou, is a small ground-dwelling bird endemic to the [[Neotropical realm|neotropics]]. It is a rarely seen bird due to its small size and discreet appearance.<ref name=":4" />

==Taxonomy==
The grey-legged tinamou was first described by John T. Zimmer in 1938.<ref name="zimmer">{{Cite journal|last1=Milensky|first1=Christopher M|last2=Hinds|first2=Wiltshire|last3=Aleixo|first3=Alexandre|title=Birds|journal=Bulletin of the Biological Society of Washington|year=2005|volume=13|page=43|doi=10.2988/0097-0298(2005)13[43:B]2.0.CO;2}}</ref> The grey-legged tinamou, like all [[Tinamou|tinamous]], is in the family tinamidae and the infraclass [[palaeognathae]].<ref name=":0">{{cite web|title=Gray-legged Tinamou (Crypturellus duidae)|url=https://www.peruaves.org/tinamidae/grey-legged-tinamou-crypturellus-duidae/|access-date=2021-11-11|website=Peru Aves}}</ref> In addition, There are 21 species in the genus ''[[Crypturellus|Crypterellus]]'', all of which are all South American tinamous.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|last1=Cabot|first1=Jose|last2=Christie|first2=David|last3=Jutglar|first3=Francesc|last4=Garcia|first4=Ernest|last5=Sharpe|first5=Christopher J.|title=Gray-legged Tinamou|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/gyltin1/cur/introduction?login|access-date=2021-11-11|website=Birds of the World|publisher=The Cornell Lab of Ornithology}}</ref> There are no subspecies of the grey-legged tinamou; it is [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]].<ref name=":2" />  The genus name, ''[[Crypturellus|Crypterellus]]'', is formed from three latin or greek words ‘[[krusptis]]’ meaning covered or hidden, ‘oura’ meaning tail and 'ellus' meaning diminutive. Therefore, crypterullus means small hidden tail.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gotch|first=Arthur Frederick|title=Latin Names Explained: A Guide to the Scientific Classification of Reptiles, Birds and Mammals|publisher=Blandford|year=1995|isbn=9780713725650|location=New York, New York}}</ref>

There is much debate in the scientific community about the membership of tinamous to the ratite clade. [[Ratite|Ratites]] are an order of large flightless birds that include [[ostrich]]s, [[Kiwi (bird)|kiwis]], [[Emu|emus]], and the extinct [[moa]] and [[elephant bird]].<ref name=":3">{{cite journal |last1=Phillips |first1=M |title=Tinamous and Moa Flock Together: Mitochondrial Genome Sequence Analysis Reveals Independent Losses of Flight among Ratites |journal=Systematic Biology |date=2010 |volume=59 |issue=1 |pages=90–107 |doi=10.1093/SYSBIO/SYP079|pmid=20525622 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Tinamous were historically considered a sister clade to ratites because they did not share the flightless quality of the rest of the group.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Prum|first1=Richard O|last2=Berv|first2=Jacob S|last3=Dornburg|first3=Alex|last4=Field|first4=Daniel J|last5=Townsend|first5=Jeffrey P|last6=Lemmon|first6=Emily Moriarty|last7=Lemmon|first7=Alan R|title=A comprehensive phylogeny of birds (Aves) using targeted next-generation DNA sequencing|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/nature15697|journal=Nature|year=2015|publication-date=2015-10-07|volume=526|issue=7574|pages=569–573|doi=10.1038/nature15697|pmid=26444237|bibcode=2015Natur.526..569P|s2cid=205246158|url-access=subscription}}</ref> However, some genetic evidence points to tinamous being close relatives of Moas, indicating that tinamous are also members of the ratite clade. This [[Phylogenetic tree|phylogeny]] suggests a novel method of the evolution of flightlessness in ratites and means that flightlessness was lost multiple times among different ratites.<ref name=":3" /> This taxonomy is, however, unresolved and requires further investigation for a conclusive answer.

==Description==
The grey-legged tinamou is a small ground-dwelling bird and a weak flyer. The grey-legged tinamou has a rusty coloured neck and upper back with a tinge of grey on its upper breast. The wings and the body of the tinamou are a scaly brownish black. The grey-legged tinamou has a pronounced black eye. They vary from 28-31 cm in height. <ref name=":0" />Males have a prominent dark crown of feathers on the top of the head, whereas females have a fully rusty coloured head. Females have markings on their backs and wings.<ref name=":2" /> The legs are a slate grey coloured which is described in the name of the species. 

The grey-legged tinamou can be misidentified with the [[variegated tinamou]]. However, the variegated tinamou has a completely grey head and bolder barring on the wings and back.<ref name=":2" />

==Distribution and habitat==

Grey-legged tinamous are native to South America, including [[Colombia|Columbia]], [[Peru]], [[Ecuador]], [[Guyana]], [[Venezuela]], and northern [[Brazil]].<ref name="foo">{{cite web |title=Grey-legged Tinamou (Crypturellus duidae) |url=https://ebird.org/species/gyltin1 |website=eBird |publisher=Cornell lab of Ornithology |access-date=2021-11-11}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{cite journal|last1=Vásquez-Arévalo|first1=Francisco A.|last2=Zárate-Gómez|first2=Ricardo|last3=Socolar|first3=Jacob B.|last4=Díazalván|first4=Juan|last5=Pérez-Peña|first5=Pedro E.|date=2020-05-18|title=First record of the gray-legged tinamou, Crypturellus duidae, and other poor-soil specialist birds from peatlands in the Putumayo River basin, Loreto, Peru|journal=Acta Amazonica|volume=50|issue=2|pages=155–158|doi=10.1590/1809-4392201901531|s2cid=219512737|doi-access=free|hdl=11250/2830588|hdl-access=free}}</ref> However, its distribution could be even further as the population is sparsely distributed and found in fragmented landscape. It is found in dry shrubland up to {{convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}} in altitude. As a poor soil specialist, the grey-legged tinamous are commonly found in Amazonian [[Sand forest|white-sand forests]]. These forests are characterized by low-hanging canopies and dense foliage, allowing the grey-legged tinamous to remain inconspicuous. They have also been documented in peatland habitats.<ref name=":1" />

==Behavior==
===Vocalization===
The call of the grey-legged tinamou is described as a short hollow sounding two-syllable whistle, with the pneumonic: “whoo-whoooooooo” with a slight rise near the end <ref name=":2" />.. The call lasts 2-3 seconds.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Alvarez Alonso|first1=José|last2=Whitney|first2=Bret M.|title=New Distributional Records of Birds From White-Sand Forests of the Northern Peruvian Amazon, With Implications for Biogeography of Northern South America|url=https://academic-oup-com.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/condor/article/105/3/552/5563354|journal=The Condor|year=2003|publication-date=2003-08-01|volume=105|issue=3|pages=552–566|doi=10.1093/condor/105.3.552|doi-access=free}}</ref> This is the only vocalization of the grey-legged tinamou recorded. See [https://ebird.org/media/catalog?taxonCode=gyltin1&sort=rating_rank_desc&mediaType=a&regionCode= Grey-legged tinamou call eBird]. Their call is most frequently at dawn and dusk, but also during the day when it is cloudy or rainy.<ref name=":4" />

===Diet===
The grey-legged tinamou primarily consumes fruits and other plant matter.<ref name=":4" /> They feed primarily on low hanging or fallen fruit. Like all tinamous they forage on the ground and in low bushes.<ref name=":2" /> They have also been documented to consume insects and other ground-dwelling invertebrates .<ref name=":4" /> 

===Reproduction===
Although there is no available information on the reproduction of the grey-legged tinamou,<ref name=":2" /> most tinamou species are ground nesters, typically choosing to nest in the depressions of the [[Buttress root|buttress roots]] of trees.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Raties: Reproduction|url=https://www.britannica.com/animal/ratite|access-date=2021-11-11|website=Britannica}}</ref>

==Conservation==
In 2020 the [[IUCN]] classified the grey-legged tinamou as [[Least Concern]], although there were calls to reclassified the species due to global population declines in the last two years in certain parts of their range.<ref name=":2" /> This is primarily due to increased threat from deforestation.<ref name=":2" /> The species is also threatened by hunting practices in the area.<ref name=":0" /> Indeed the regions the grey-legged tinamou is most commonly found is facing significant anthropogenic changes. although due to their large fragmented range there was yet to be detailed survey of their populations across their entire range.

zh.extract:
灰腿穴䳍（台湾作灰足穴䳍）（学名：Crypturellus duidae）是䳍科穴䳍屬的一种鸟类。


特征
灰腿穴䳍体重约426克，翼长约162.9毫米，嘴峰长约31.2毫米，喙宽度约5.4毫米，喙厚度约5.6毫米，跗蹠长约48.9毫米，尾长约41.5毫米。灰腿穴䳍在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的灌木丛中。食性为草食性。


分布
哥伦比亚中东部至委内瑞拉南部的热带森林。
